title: Efficient ultrafast homodyne detection of quantum light
authors: Young-Do Yoon, Chan Roh, Geunhee Gwak, Young-Sik Ra
abstract: Ultrafast continuous-variable quantum states offer new opportunities for advanced quantum technologies, but efficient homodyne detection of these states remains challenging. Here, we present a method for efficient ultrafast homodyne detection by exploiting temporal correlations in detector signals. By optimizing the temporal weight used to extract quadrature outcomes, we achieve a substantial increase in the signal-to-noise ratio of ultrafast homodyne detection, thereby improving the detection efficiency. We analyze the autocorrelations of shot noise and electronic noise and determine the optimal weight by solving a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem. The optimal weight enhances the squeezing and anti-squeezing levels observed experimentally. These results highlight the importance of optimized signal processing for efficient quantum measurements.
summary: The paper presents a method to improve the detection efficiency of ultrafast quantum light by optimizing the temporal weights used in homodyne detection. By accounting for detector response and electronic noise, the authors significantly enhance the observable squeezing and non-Gaussianity of pulsed quantum states.
main_problem: Efficient homodyne detection of ultrafast continuous-variable quantum states is limited by slow detector response and electronic noise, which degrades the signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) and obscures nonclassical features.
main_result: By optimizing a temporal weight function using a generalized Rayleigh quotient approach, the authors achieved a 135% increase in SNR compared to constant weighting, leading to enhanced observation of squeezing and Wigner-function negativity.
method: The authors formulate the optimization of the temporal weight as a generalized Rayleigh quotient problem to maximize SNR, utilizing a truncated Fourier-basis to regularize against sampling errors.
model_or_system: An experimental ultrafast homodyne detection setup using a Ti:sapphire laser, a spontaneously pumped optical parametric oscillator (SPOPO) for squeezed vacuum, and a transimpedlamperance amplifier-based detector.
key_observables: Signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), detection efficiency, squeezing and anti-squeezing levels, Wigner-function negativity, and noise statistics (skewness and kurtosis).
important_parameters: 80 MHz repetition rate, 75 fs pulse duration, 800 nm wavelength, 10 GSa/s sampling rate, and 13-18 dB SNR range.
main_assumptions: Electronic noise is assumed to be stationary; the detector response is modeled via a circuit-based impulse response.
figures_summary: Fig 1: Detection scheme; Fig 2: Enhancement of nonclassical features vs SNR; Fig 3: Experimental setup; Fig 4: Detector characterization (power spectra and pulse response); Fig 5: Autocorrelation matrices; Fig 6: Performance comparison of weight vectors; Fig 7: Histograms of quadrature outcomes.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the challenge of ultrafast detection, presents a mathematical optimization framework for temporal weighting, details the experimental implementation and detector characterization, and concludes with a performance comparison of different weighting strategies and noise statistics.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant to quantum optics and photonic quantum information as it provides a practical method to recover high-fidelity quantum states from noisy, high-speed experimental measurements.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Ultrafast homodyne detection. (a) An input field interferes with the local oscillator (LO) at the 50:50 beam splitter, and two photodiodes detect the output beams and convert them into photocurrents ˆi±(t). The photocurrent dif- ference ˆi(t) = ˆi+(t) −ˆi−(t) is converted into a voltage sig- nal ˆv(t) by the transimpedance amplifier. (b) A homodyne detection signal and its processing for obtaining quadrature outcomes. ˆ X(j) shows the quadrature outcome for the j-th pulse, and αLO(t) depicts the time-varying amplitude of the LO, which is formed by a train of ultrafast pulses with repe- tition period T. The resulting voltage signal ˆv(t) is weighted by the weight function w(t) and...

FIG. 2. Enhancement of observable nonclassical features with increasing SNR, expressed in dB. The enhancement is defined relative to the value obtained at an initial SNR of 13 dB. (a,b) Squeezing enhancement ∆Ssq in dB for (a) various squeezing parameters r at fixed residual efficiency η0 = 0.976 and (b) various values of η0 at fixed r = 2.7. (c) Enhancement of the Wigner-function negativity at the origin, ∆W(0, 0), for a single-photon state. The quantity is plotted as 2π∆W(0, 0); on this scale, a pure single-photon state has 2πW(0, 0) = −1.

FIG. 3. Experimental setup. A Ti:sapphire laser (75 fs pulse width, 80 MHz repetition rate, 800 nm central wavelength) is employed as an ultrafast light source. A portion of the light is converted to 400 nm via SHG and used as the pump for the SPOPO to generate a pulsed squeezed state. A weak coherent seed beam is additionally injected into the SPOPO to lock the relative phase between the squeezed state and the LO. The homodyne detector measures the quadratures of the vacuum or squeezed vacuum.

FIG. 4. Homodyne detector characteristics. (a) Power spec- trum of shot noise for 4 mW LO power (red) and of electronic noise (gray) measured with a spectrum analyzer in the range of 10 kHz to 300 MHz. (b) Example of 500 shot-noise pulses rearranged into five-period blocks and overlaid. (c) Shot-noise matrix S for 4 mW LO power over five periods.

FIG. 5. Analysis of homodyne detector signals. (a)–(c) Autocorrelation matrices: (a) electronic-noise matrix E, (b) shot-noise matrix S, and (c) detector response matrix R. Matrices in (b) and (c) are obtained at an LO power of 4 mW, and all matrices are determined from 80,000 repeated measurements. (d)–(f) Eigenvalues of (d) E, (e) S, and (f) R, shown in descending order. (g)–(i) The three leading eigenvectors of (g) E, (h) S, and (i) R.
